sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 28, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ** - Board has a gray background with two white panels separated by a purple vertical bar - Left panel: dark gray box (with white interior) at lower-left, light green square with white center pixel - Between panels: light green square with black center pixel, white square - Right panel: dark gray box (with white interior) at upper area - Two green squares (same hash) appear to be agent-like objects with different inner colors (W vs B) - Goal likely involves moving green squares to interact with boxes or cross the purple barrier
- Plan: ** Try MOUSE to click on objects, or try moving green squares to box positions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 27 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, the right green square is now next to the right box. The left green square is in the middle area. Let me check exact positions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check positions after 5 RIGHTs
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All relevant objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'c'] and n['pixels'] < 30:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, bounds: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, children={n['children']}")
# Right box interior is at rows 27-29, cols 45-47
# Right green needs to be at rows 27-29, cols 45-47 to be inside
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
All relevant objects:
  id=4, color=c, bounds: rows 26-30, cols 44-48, children=[5]
  id=6, color=N, bounds: rows 30-32, cols 27-29, children=[9]
  id=8, color=N, bounds: rows 30-32, cols 42-44, children=[10]
  id=11, color=c, bounds: rows 32-36, cols 11-15, children=[12]

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see:
- Left green (id=6): rows 30-32, cols 27-29
- Right green (id=8): rows 30-32, cols 42-44
- Right box (id=4): rows 26-30, cols 44-48, children=[5]
- Left box (id=11): rows 32-36, cols 11-15, children=[12]
The right green square is at cols 42-44, and the right box is at cols 44-48. The right green is adjacent to the right box but not inside yet. The right box interior is at rows 27-29, cols 45-47.
The right green is at rows 30-32, cols 42-44. To get inside the right box:
- Need to move RIGHT by 3 cells (1 RIGHT) to get to cols 45-47
- Need to move UP by 3 cells (1 UP) to get to rows 27-29
But both greens are at the same row, so both would move. Let me try RIGHT first, then UP.
Wait, but the left green is at cols 27-29 and the left box is at cols 11-15. If I move RIGHT, the left green moves further away from the left box.
Actually, let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is just to get the right green square into the right box, and the left green square's position doesn't matter. Or maybe I need to get both green squares into their respective boxes.
Looking at the image, the right green square is right next to the right box. Let me try RIGHT to push it inside, then UP to center it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Right green at rows 30-32, cols 42-44
# Right box interior at rows 27-29, cols 45-47
# Need RIGHT 1 (to cols 45-47) then UP 1 (to rows 27-29)
# Both greens are at same row so both move
action(['RIGHT', 'UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
